data.title: OpenClaw founder joins OpenAI: To create AI that even moms can use.
data.description:
Written by: Long Yue, Wall Street Insights

The battle for AI talent in Silicon Valley has escalated. With Zuckerberg and Altman personally vying for the role, the founder of the wildly popular AI agent OpenClaw has ultimately sided with OpenAI, aiming to overcome the final hurdle in making AI truly "fun" and "useful."

OpenAI CEO Sam Altman officially confirmed on Sunday via social media platform X that Peter Steinberger, the creator of the open-source AI agent OpenClaw, will join the company.

Regarding the project's ownership, which has been a focus of market attention, both parties have reassured the public: OpenClaw will not be privatized but will instead be transferred to an independent foundation to continue open-sourcing, with OpenAI committing to providing support.

Altman: Steinberger is a genius. Altman highly praised Steinberger's joining and directly pointed out OpenAI's next product strategy direction. "Peter Steinberger joined OpenAI to drive the development of the next generation of personal agents," Altman wrote. "We expect this to soon become central to our product offerings." This signifies that OpenAI's strategic focus is shifting from single, large-scale model capabilities to more complex agent interactions. Altman further explained the underlying logic: "He (Steinberger) is a genius with many amazing ideas about how highly intelligent agents will interact in the future to do things that are extremely useful to humanity." He emphasized, "The future will be an extremely multi-agent world, and supporting open source is important to us as part of that." From "Geek Toys" to "Mass Consumer Products": Even Moms Can Use It. Steinberger's core task after joining OpenAI is very clear and straightforward: to solve the usability of AI. OpenClaw previously gained immense popularity on GitHub for its ability to autonomously control mobile phones, clean emails, and book flights, but its operational barrier remains high. Steinberger stated, "My next mission is to build an intelligent agent that even my mother can use. This requires broader changes, more thinking about how to achieve this safely, and access to the latest models and research." This statement addresses the biggest pain point in the current AI market. While large models are powerful, they lack an interactive layer that allows ordinary consumers to use them with "zero barriers." OpenAI's move aims to fill this gap, pushing AI agents from the developer community to billions of ordinary users, which directly relates to the ceiling of AI commercialization and monetization.

Altman's "Computing Power Strategy" vs. Zuckerberg's "Geek Passion"

This is not just a job offer, but a microcosm of a giant game. Meta and OpenAI launched drastically different offensives to win over Steinberger.

It is revealed that Meta played the "direct hiring by CEO" card. Zuckerberg not only personally tried the product, but even talked to Steinberger on the phone to discuss code details, trying to impress him with "technical resonance." Steinberger once commented: "Zuckerberg understands me."

But in the end, OpenAI won this round with absolute "hard power".

Altman directly demonstrated the top-tier computing power potential brought by the Cerebras collaboration and the deep support for the Codex model. In current AI R&D, computing power is the ultimate measure. Steinberger frankly stated, "Ultimately, I feel that OpenAI is the best place to continue advancing my vision and expanding its influence." This sends a signal to investors: in the competition for top AI talent, simply offering high salaries or CEO favoritism is no longer the deciding factor; whoever can provide the most cutting-edge model access and computing resources has the pricing power. The "Fatal Triad" and the Demise of Apps: With the exponential leap in the capabilities of intelligent agents, security and the restructuring of business models have become the B-side of market attention. OpenClaw once "went crazy" due to excessive permissions, sending hundreds of spam messages to users. Cybersecurity experts call this combination of "access to private data + external communication capabilities + exposure to untrusted content" the "Lethal Trifecta." After Steinberger joined the company, how to grant AI autonomy while keeping it under control will be OpenAI's primary technical challenge. The more profound impact lies in the software ecosystem. Steinberger predicts that in the future, "80% of apps will disappear," existing instead as APIs, with intelligent agents taking over all interactions. If this prediction comes true, existing traffic distribution logic and app advertising models will be completely overturned. For investors, the focus should not only be on model vendors, but also on companies that are building "agent-friendly" API interfaces.

data.title: Is X not only going to be a social platform, but also trying to steal business from exchanges?
data.description:
When you see $BTC or $TSLA while scrolling through tweets, perhaps in a few weeks, clicking these tags will allow you to directly place orders to buy or sell. The social platform X (formerly Twitter) is moving from concept to reality in its pursuit of a "social + trading" ecosystem. Recently, Nikita Bier, X's product manager, has repeatedly stated publicly that the platform will launch a new feature called "Smart Cashtags" in the coming weeks, allowing users to trade stocks and cryptocurrencies directly within their timeline. This news quickly ignited discussions in the crypto and fintech communities. But is X really going to become an exchange? The matter is more complex and thought-provoking than it appears on the surface.

01 Smart Cashtags: From "Hyperlinks" to "Transaction Entry Points"

According to Nikita Bier's demonstration and description, Smart Cashtags are far more than simply adding hyperlinks to code symbols.

According to Nikita Bier's demonstration and description, Smart Cashtags are far more than just adding hyperlinks to code symbols.

Precise Asset Anchoring: In the cryptocurrency market, the proliferation of tokens with the same name has always been a pain point—one $DOGE might have dozens of copycats. Smart Cashtags allow posters to specify a specific smart contract address for the tag, meaning that clicking the tag will directly point to a unique and accurate target asset. This is crucial for preventing users from "buying the wrong coin." Real-time Market Data Embedded: When a user clicks on a tab like $BTC or $NVDA in the timeline, a real-time price chart, related discussions about the asset, and most importantly, a "Buy/Sell" button will pop up directly. A Prototype of a Trading Loop: In the screenshot shown by Nikita Bier, opening the NVDA tab reveals a one-click buy or sell option. While it's unclear whether this feature will cover all crypto assets, it sends a strong signal: X is attempting to embed trading functionality directly into its news feed. The traditional financial decision-making process is usually: see news → switch apps to search → check market data → find analysis → make a trading decision. Smart Cashtags attempts to compress this into: see news/discussion → click on tags (check market data/view public opinion) → one-click trading. When information flow and price flow are combined, social platforms take a crucial step in evolving into financial terminals.

02 X's Bigger Strategy: From "Social Square" to "Everything App"

Smart Cashtags are not an isolated feature; they are a key component of X's strategy to build an "Everything App."

X Money is poised for launch: At the xAI launch event, Musk himself revealed that X's internal payment system, X Money, has completed internal closed testing and is expected to launch a limited external beta version within the next 1 to 2 months, followed by a gradual rollout to global users.

This feature is positioned as a core financial module within the X ecosystem, supporting in-app peer-to-peer transfers and various currency transaction scenarios. Visa Support: X has entered into a strategic partnership with Visa, supporting real-time fiat currency top-ups, debit card payments, and bank transfers through the Visa Direct network. This paves the way for future transaction functions. Licensing Progress: To date, X Payments LLC has obtained money transfer licenses in over 40 states in the United States. While approvals in key regions such as New York State are yet to be granted, the overall compliance framework is gradually being improved.

Data + Trading Entry：Smart Cashtags integrates real-time market data, enabling the platform to possess the dual capabilities of providing financial information and serving as an entry point for trading decisions. Nikita Bier stated bluntly: "X is the best platform for obtaining financial information—the content users read here influences the allocation of trillions of dollars in funds." If social networking, payments, market data, and trading are all integrated, X could indeed become a super ecosystem tool similar to China's "WeChat + Payment + Investment" model. 03 Market Reaction and Industry Interpretation Following the news, the crypto market quickly responded. DOGE Rises in Response: As Elon Musk's "golden child" repeatedly publicly supported, Dogecoin (DOGE) experienced significant price fluctuations during the news's release, with a short-term increase reaching 11%. Market sentiment interpreted this as a prelude to the realization of positive news. Solana's Influence: Notably, Nikita Bier, besides being the product manager for X, also holds a special position—strategic advisor to Solana Labs. In the Smart Cashtags screenshot he showed, the dropdown list after entering "$B" included BTC and BONK, a leading meme coin in the Solana ecosystem. The community generally believes this is not a coincidence, but reflects a preference for and intention to promote the Solana ecosystem. Solana is known for its speed and extremely low transaction fees, which perfectly matches the needs of high-frequency micro-payments in social finance. The same screenshot also shows information about the Base token, which some in the community interpret as an "abstract prophecy" that Base is about to issue its own token. While this remains speculative at present, it reflects the market's rich imagination regarding potential collaborations between X and various public chains. 04 Official Clarification: Is X Really an Exchange? Strictly speaking: No. Faced with the market's enthusiastic expectations, X officials quickly clarified their position: "X will not be responsible for trade execution or act as a broker; it is merely building financial data tools and links," Nikita Bier stated explicitly. This means that current Smart Cashtags are more accurately positioned as a transaction entry point or transaction overlay. The actual core financial functions, such as transaction execution, asset custody, and order matching, may be completed through third-party partners, or gradually implemented in the future through the X Money system. This "platform provides the stage, partners perform" model allows X to avoid complex financial regulatory licensing requirements in the early stages, while quickly launching functions and validating market responses.

05 Potential Challenges: Regulation and Risk

Despite the promising vision, X still faces several hurdles in order to roll out its trading functionality globally.

Regulatory Obstacles: New York State is one of the most strictly regulated financial jurisdictions in the United States, and X has not yet obtained a money transmission license in New York State, having withdrawn its application there in 2024. This means that in the foreseeable future,New York users may not be able to use X's payment and trading functions.

Side effects of socially driven trading: When KOLs tweet to recommend a small-cap coin, and followers can buy it with just a "one-click" click, FOMO (Fear of Missing Out) is easily amplified instantly. This could make Pump and Dump (market manipulation) easier than ever before. How to limit malicious tags and prevent market manipulation will be a huge challenge. Data Source Reliability: For cryptocurrencies, where do real-time prices come from? Centralized exchanges or decentralized oracles? The credibility and resistance to manipulation of the data source directly affect user experience and platform reputation. New Challenges for Account Security: When social media accounts are directly linked to wallets, the incentive for hackers to attack will increase exponentially. X needs to invest significant resources in content moderation, fraud prevention, and account security.

06 Conclusion

From Bluebird to X, from social square to financial gateway, Musk is gradually turning his dream blueprint of the "universal app" into reality.

The launch of Smart Cashtags marks the official arrival of the era of "socially driven transactions". For the 600 million X users worldwide, a few weeks later, when you see that tweet with the "$" symbol again, you may face a completely new choice: to simply "like" it, or to directly "buy".

06 Conclusion

From Bluebird to X, from social square to financial gateway, Musk is gradually turning his dream blueprint of the "universal app" into reality.

The launch of Smart Cashtags marks the official arrival of the era of "socially driven transactions".For the 600 million X users worldwide, a few weeks later, when you see that tweet with the "$" symbol again, you may face a completely new choice: to simply "like" it, or to directly "buy".

06 Conclusion

From Bluebird to X, from social square to financial gateway, Musk is gradually turning his dream blueprint of the "universal app" into reality. Whoever controls the flow of financial information controls the gateway to capital flows. When X completes its closed loop of "information + payment + transaction," it will become one of the new infrastructures of the global financial market. At that time, the allocation decisions of trillions of dollars will not only be based on information on X, but will occur directly on X. Do you think that direct trading on X will be a breakthrough moment for cryptocurrency adoption, or will it be a case of "much ado about nothing" due to regulatory and risk issues? Feel free to leave your comments below.

data.title: The father of OpenClaw joins OpenAI; Ultraman gets his hands on it.
data.description:
The Spring Festival is a joyous occasion, and the AI ​​Agent community has witnessed a major personnel change.

Unexpectedly, OpenClaw (formerly Clawdbot/Moltbot) went from explosive popularity to joining OpenAI in just one month.

Just now, Peter Steinberger, the father of OpenClaw, announced that he has joined OpenAI, and OpenClaw will become an open and independent foundation.

OpenAI's Sam Altman also announced at X that with Peter Steinberger joining, they will be working on the next generation of personal assistant agents.

"The claw is the law."

OpenClaw focuses on one keyword: agent AI. To its fans, it's not just an ordinary chatbot, but a hands-on personal assistant capable of handling tasks such as automated email processing, communication with insurance companies, flight check-in, and multi-step online tasks. Since its release last November, the project has experienced explosive growth, boasting over 100,000 stars on GitHub and 2 million weekly visits, achieving near-viral success within the AI ​​community. This personnel change sends a strong signal: OpenAI is seriously betting on the personal agent track. Over the past year, the industry's main themes have evolved: the Chatbot boom of 2023, the toolification of Copilot in 2024, and the autonomous execution of Agents in 2025-2026. The entry of OpenClaw this time likely signifies the next stage of competition: from "AI that can talk" to "AI that can do things for you." Following OpenClaw's explosive popularity, social intelligent agents have become a hot topic in AI this year. Perhaps many startups targeting this direction will emerge in the future.

data.title: Starting with HyperLiquid: What kind of exchange does RWA really need?
data.description: The real solution lies in structural shifts, not emotional venting; in acceptance, not fawning. The next stage for RWA is not a flashpoint, but an entry point; not a traffic source, but an institutional one. The hottest trends of 2025 were undoubtedly HyperLiquid and Aster. There are many explanations for their explosive popularity, and the angles offered are often quite unconventional. However, the fundamental reasons for their success might be easier to understand by examining them from a product perspective. After this analysis, can we extend this to RWA DEX? If so, how should we upgrade and develop them? This article will try to explain this clearly. Understanding the Essence of HyperLiquid and Aster's Sudden Popularity The fundamental reason for the explosive popularity of Aster and Hyperliquid can be summarized in one sentence: They are not "better DEXs," but rather "the first to put the **sovereignty** of exchanges on-chain." Simply put, from a product perspective, it's not because of performance, transaction fees, or UI/UX. Rather, it's because of a structural change in "who controls the transactions." Why did HyperLiquid become so popular? You've probably heard these things: self-developed L1, high performance; CLOB is like a CEX, low latency, good depth, excellent user experience; but these only explain "easy to use," not "explosive popularity." After in-depth research on HyperLiquid by Go2Mars PRI (Product Research Institute), a conclusion was reached: HyperLiquid's real breakthrough: it changed "transaction sovereignty." In traditional CEXs/DEXs, the platform holds the actual control over all aspects of trading, including listing, delisting, risk control, liquidation logic, rule changes, and trading suspensions. In other words, "users participating in trading are merely passive participants." What did Hyperliquid do? It broke down the "core power of the exchange" into modules that can be constrained by on-chain rules. The key is not "decentralization," but rather: whether the rules can be unilaterally modified and whether they can be subject to human intervention in extreme cases; Hyperliquid's core message is: "Even the system itself cannot arbitrarily change the rules." In 2025 and earlier, a certain type of thing frequently occurred: too many transaction interventions under the guise of "compliance/risk control/risk management." The result of this type of transaction is that profits are rolled back, positions are forcibly liquidated, the market is suspended, and rules are retroactively modified. This makes high-frequency trading/institutional/smart money realize for the first time that they are bearing "institutional risk," not "market risk." Hyperliquid's essential appeal is that "I only bear market risk, not the platform's will." This is a qualitative change in the product itself. Therefore, Hyperliquid's explosive growth wasn't due to a surge in user numbers, but rather: the migration of professional traders, large funds willing to operate without a physical presence, the ability to deploy strategies long-term, and extremely high system predictability—this is the on-chain realization of "exchange credibility." We can clearly see one thing: Aster's explosive popularity is different from Hyperliquid's. On the surface, Aster's product appears to be a next-generation derivatives DEX, modular, with a good UX and innovative mechanism design, but these are not its core strengths. Aster's real breakthrough lies in "the abstract upgrade of trading behavior." In short, Aster doesn't sell trading itself, but rather "the encapsulation of trading capabilities." Traditional exchanges give users the right to place orders, cancel orders, and leverage; Aster, on the other hand, gives users strategy-level interfaces, conditional execution, risk structure templates, and behavioral combination permissions. Simply put, users aren't "trading," but rather: invoke a set of "market behavior capabilities." The reason Aster has become so popular is essentially because the user base has changed. Most users are novices or gamblers, but rather "strategy users/agents/automated systems." Trading is no longer manual but systematic. Aster essentially provides a "legal, stable, and composable trading execution environment" for AI/Bots/Agents/quantitative trading. Inspiration from Hyperliquid and Aster Products: Can these types of products continue to exist? The answer is yes, of course, but not by simply copying them. What can continue is not the form, but three underlying logics: transaction sovereignty must be verifiable, transactions are not "page behavior" but "system capabilities," and exchanges themselves are "institutional products." Hyperliquid actually solves the problem of "untrustworthy institutions," addressing the question: Will the platform change its rules? Aster solves the problem of "insufficient abstraction of trading capabilities," addressing the question: Can transactions be invoked by the system? Previously, in an article we published on Go2Mars PRI titled "Web 3 is Entering a Rule-Generating Phase," we discussed how the next stage of Web 3 is not a period of explosive growth, but rather an entry point; not a traffic source, but a regulatory one. Since then, we have a basic understanding of the fundamental reasons for the explosive popularity of Hyperliquid and Aster. So, can we use this logic to return to the RWA sector, which has been hyped for over two years, and discuss the future direction of RWA exchanges? Does an RWA exchange exist? Strictly speaking, a "true RWA exchange" almost doesn't exist yet. Why do the so-called RWA DEX/CEXs we see now "not resemble exchanges"? Because they are mostly stuck on three things: unclear legal responsibility, an unclosed loop in clearing and execution, and unnatural liquidity. Let's explain these three issues separately: **Unclear Legal Responsibility:** Who is the issuer, who guarantees authenticity, and who is responsible for default? These are all unclear. **Incomplete Liquidation and Execution:** On-chain transactions, off-chain non-compliance, ultimately relying on the law, and finally, the on-chain rules become ineffective, making it a complete joke. **Unnatural Liquidity:** There is no market making, no continuous pricing, and it's more like a "private placement." Based on research from Go2Mars PRI and historical review, we believe a "true RWA exchange" must possess: on-chain liquidation rights > off-chain ownership, automated default handling, and RWA itself being a "cash flow instrument," not "proof of assets." We explain these three fundamental principles: On-chain liquidation rights > off-chain ownership: It's not about "I own this asset," but rather: "When a rule is triggered, I have the right to execute a certain result." For example: priority of returns, right to dispose of collateral, and right to allocate cash flow. Default is automated: Default enforcement here certainly doesn't rely on laws or courts, but rather on: pledges, margin deposits, risk pools, and advance compensation, bringing the cost of default forward, rather than pursuing accountability afterward. RWA itself is a "cash flow instrument," not an "asset certificate": What RWA can trade is not "houses/debts," but rather the "priority of cash flow." The priority of cash flow refers to the agreement on who receives the money first, how much, and how much risk they bear. The core lies in the recombination of risk and return, and the priority of cash flow is arguably the most important aspect of RWA. So, based on the current situation, are there any products that are "close to the correct form"? The answer is yes, but they are all still in the "half-finished product" stage. They typically manifest as follows: they are not called exchanges, nor do they emphasize RWA, but they are already implementing on-chain cash flow allocation, risk stratification, and automatic liquidation. Therefore, the true RWA exchange in the future may not be called an RWA exchange. For RWA and the RWA exchange, the key issue isn't simply "asset on-chain," as that's a very simple matter. The real challenge lies in "on-chaining the systems of responsibility, liquidation, and default." Can default, enforcement, and cash flow prioritization be implemented programmatically? In conclusion, RWA's ultimate goal isn't "asset on-chain," but "system on-chain." Looking back at the explosive success of Hyperliquid and Aster, they weren't essentially "creating a better exchange," but rather accomplishing something deeper—transforming exchange rules into on-chain regulations. Hyperliquid addresses the question: Will the platform change its rules? Aster addresses the question: Can transactions be invoked by the system? A true RWA exchange needs to solve a much more difficult problem: Can default, liability, and cash flow prioritization be handled programmatically? If this problem cannot be solved, RWA will forever remain an "asset presentation layer"; only if this problem is solved will RWA become an "institutional financial layer." Over the past two years, the market has focused on "how to put assets on-chain"—real estate, debt, bills, fund shares, income rights, mining farms, power plants… But these are merely superficial. The truly valuable aspect is not asset proof, but rather the execution structure of cash flow. Who allocates first? Who bears the first loss? What are the default trigger conditions? Is execution automatic? Is liquidation irreversible? These problems are essentially "systemic issues," not "asset issues." If defaults still require court intervention, if performance still relies on human judgment, and if liquidation can still be negotiated and modified—then the so-called RWA DEX is merely a traditional financial product with a blockchain UI. That's not an upgrade, but a repackaging. A true RWA exchange may not look like what we're familiar with. It may not emphasize "decentralization," may not focus on "a wide variety of assets," and may not even be called an "exchange." But it must possess three things: Rules precede asset existence, liquidation weight precedes ownership, and default costs are upfront, not retroactive; When these conditions are met, RWA is not just an "on-chain private placement," but a composable cash flow market. At that point, the object of trading is no longer "a specific project," but "a certain risk structure." It's not "buying assets," but "buying the right to prioritize a segment of cash flow." If Web3 is entering a "rule-generating phase," then RWA's mission is to transform the most core, hidden, and artificial aspects of traditional finance—default handling and profit ranking—into verifiable, composable, and executable procedural structures. When the system itself becomes a product, when liquidation logic becomes an interface, and when risk structures can be assembled like Lego bricks, RWA will truly become a new financial paradigm, not just a shell of the old. Perhaps the real RWA exchange won't explode in terms of "asset size," but rather attract capital through "system credibility." Just as Hyperliquid attracted the migration of professional traders, the future RWA structured market will attract: capital unwilling to bear systemic risks, institutions desiring transparent risk structures, and AI/Agent/quantitative systems needing programmable cash flow. When cash flow can be understood by algorithms, when defaults can be automatically executed, and when liquidation can be pre-priced, that will be the true breakthrough for RWA. So, the question isn't: Can RWA become an exchange? Rather: Who can be the first to thoroughly enshrine "responsibility, default, and liquidation" into on-chain rules? When that day comes, RWA will no longer be a narrative component, but a new institutional financial foundation. That will be the true upgrade and derivative. Conclusion: RWA's ultimate goal is not "asset on-chain," but "institutional on-chain." When we look back at the explosive popularity of Hyperliquid and Aster, they weren't essentially "making a better exchange," but rather accomplishing something deeper—turning exchange rules into on-chain rules. Hyperliquid addresses the question: Will the platform change its rules? Aster addresses the question: Can transactions be invoked by the system? That's not an upgrade, but a repackaging. A true RWA exchange might not look like what we're familiar with. It may not emphasize "decentralization," may not focus on "a wide variety of assets," and may not even be called an "exchange." But it must possess three things: rules precede asset existence, liquidation weight precedes ownership, and default costs are upfront, rather than retroactive accountability. When these conditions are met, RWA is not just an "on-chain private placement," but a composable cash flow market. At that point, the object of trading is no longer "a specific project," but "a certain risk structure." It's not "buying assets," but "buying the right to prioritize a segment of cash flow." data.title: The consensus reached at the Hong Kong Consensus Conference: Is the cryptocurrency industry doomed?
data.description: <div class="share___SVvtl"><div>This episode of Wu Shuo's AMA focused on observations and feelings about the Hong Kong Consensus Conference, discussing the current state and future of the cryptocurrency industry. Guests included Colin Wu, editor-in-chief of Wu Shuo; Teddy, founder of XHunt and a DeFi expert; Leon Liu, CEO & CTO of Hubble AI; Esther, head of the Hong Kong ecosystem at Conflux; Haotian, a Web3 researcher; Albert Luxon, PM at a macro hedge fund; Cindy from JiaMi Technology; and well-known cryptocurrency KOLs such as Nana and Kirara. Guests shared their views on the future of the cryptocurrency industry and their feelings about the conference. With the market downturn and the rise of AI technology, the cryptocurrency industry is experiencing emotional fluctuations, and many are beginning to feel pessimistic about the industry's prospects. The guests generally believed that the "consensus" in the cryptocurrency industry has changed, and many traditional cryptocurrency projects are facing new challenges. Despite this, some believe that the notion of "the crypto world is finished" is overly pessimistic, and that the industry still has potential, especially in areas such as payments, compliance, and stablecoins. The combination of AI and Web3 is considered a crucial future development direction, particularly the fusion of decentralization and the AI ​​economy, which may bring new opportunities. Furthermore, the speakers shared many interesting and absurd anecdotes about the conference. Overall, although the market currently faces challenges, many participants remain hopeful for the future, looking forward to finding new development directions and potential growth areas. The speakers' statements do not represent Wu Shuo's views and do not constitute any investment advice. Please strictly abide by local laws and regulations. The audio transcription was done by GPT and may contain errors. Please listen to the full podcast on platforms such as Xiaoyuzhou and YouTube. Part 1: Sharing My Impressions of the Hong Kong Consensus Conference Colin: The Hong Kong government attaches great importance to the Consensus Conference, but it lacks industry hot topics and the atmosphere at the venue is rather cold. Colin: I didn't actually participate much in the event itself, but I can share a few observations. First, I also tweeted that day, and a friend joked that this was the first time I'd encountered an event where I had to buy my own alcohol—going to a bar and having to pay for it myself. I saw quite a few people saying the same thing in the comments, which I found quite amusing. To some extent, this also reflects that the Consensus Conference as a whole was a bit lackluster. The second point is an interesting observation. I think the Hong Kong government attaches great importance to this consensus conference. Perhaps they believe Hong Kong's international role has been relatively diminished, so they particularly value large-scale conferences initiated by overseas project teams. You see, many big names spoke at the conference, and several new policies were even announced. Judging from the overall atmosphere, the Hong Kong government does indeed attach great importance to this consensus conference. The third impression is that many foreigners did come. I saw Joseph Lubin and other representatives from overseas project teams in Hong Kong. However, overall, it feels like there weren't many truly grounded, practical issues. While the Hong Kong government attaches great importance to it, there are relatively few issues that reach the forefront of the industry. On the one hand, there aren't many hot topics in the industry as a whole at present. Two major current hot topics are prediction markets and stock tokenization. However, both face significant regulatory hurdles in Hong Kong. Therefore, despite their global popularity, these topics saw little practical implementation or in-depth discussion at this Consensus Conference in Hong Kong. Another point is the recent release of policies regarding RWA (Real-World Asset On-Chain) in mainland China. I noticed this topic was discussed extensively, including by figures like Xiao Feng. However, given that this Consensus Conference primarily featured overseas organizers and participants, the discussion on new developments in RWA in mainland China and Hong Kong was relatively low. Perhaps in a few months, at events hosted by companies like Wanxiang, there will be more discussion on these developments. Haotian: From "Everyone Talking Their Own Talk" to "Collective Pessimism": The Shift in Industry Sentiment at This Year's Consensus Conference. Haotian: At the last Consensus Conference, my most direct feeling was that everyone was talking about their own thing. For example, those involved in DeFi were shouting about RWA, or some were talking about AI, some were talking about community, and various sectors were all talking about it. But you would find that everyone was indeed doing their own thing, without forming a real consensus or a collective force. There was no major trend that everyone generally recognized and was optimistic about; that was my feeling last time. But this time, what I felt was that there was a "consensus"—that is, "the crypto world is finished" and "this industry is doomed." A unified sense of pessimism and cynicism pervaded the conference, which was a strong feeling I had. Of course, this doesn't represent my personal opinion. However, I believe some areas do face the risk of "shrinking" or even "dying out," such as crypto native listing, which specifically involves listing and issuing tokens on crypto exchanges. I spoke with some relatively stable project teams recently, and their thinking is very clear: if a project grows large, their first priority won't be listing on a token, but rather going public, such as on Nasdaq or the Hong Kong Stock Exchange. This is why many people from traditional exchanges have been particularly active this time. Nasdaq, the NYSE, and the ICE system are all promoting asset tokenization. If traditional finance can provide stronger credit backing, then excellent projects will naturally be more willing to go through the sponsorship of the eight major investment banks and list on the US stock market. Listing on the US stock market provides significantly better valuation, liquidity, and long-term funding support than simply listing on a cryptocurrency exchange. This will bring about a crucial shift in perception: projects listed on the US stock market are considered "good projects," while those not listed on traditional capital markets may be labeled as risky. I even spoke with Bloomberg, and South Korean retail investors are now more inclined to buy crypto-related stocks listed on the US stock market than projects simply listed on exchanges. Traffic on South Korea's four major local exchanges has declined significantly; even the most speculative retail investors are losing interest in crypto-only listings. This means the future role of exchanges will change. Previously, exchanges could reap substantial profits through primary and secondary listings; in the future, they are more likely to revert to providing secondary service, such as transaction fees, custody, and fiat currency access. Like Coinbase, whose core advantage is ETF custody; Kraken, whose advantage is Euro fiat currency deposits and withdrawals and foreign exchange channels. In the next three to five years, the status of each exchange will largely depend on their capabilities in secondary market services. So, to summarize: the crypto market isn't finished, it's maturing. But what does maturity mean? It means less exorbitant profits, less innovation, and the intervention of traditional financial forces. Some old models, such as those relying solely on crypto native listings, may face profound changes, or even marginalization. The industry can survive, and will survive even more stably, but the days of champagne and sports cars are probably gone forever. Instead of building a liquidity loop around native on-chain assets, everyone is going their own way. This makes the "general rise logic" of native on-chain assets increasingly difficult. Hyperliquid, for example, is one of the few projects that has succeeded in this round. However, it's far removed from the market, focusing solely on product development, and unable to leverage KOLs. Such teams are rare. The same applies to prediction markets. Polymarket dominates MindShares, with valuations often reaching $30 billion. How do newcomers price their offerings? You can't claim to be anchored to Polymarket; that would be ridiculed. Many native prediction markets in the crypto world are essentially just about leveraging liquidity. Looking at the technical narrative, ZK, high-performance Layer 1, and BTC Layer 2 are almost never discussed anymore. There's a sense of tragic grandeur, like the mighty Yangtze flowing eastward. The founders are still working diligently, but seem to have been abandoned by the times. Many problems exist, but are there new opportunities? I've been talking about a direction lately—the Agentic Economy. Why do I think this is an opportunity? Because AI consensus is taking shape. Innovation is still happening. Things like new ERC standards, the expansion of stablecoins, prediction markets, AI trading, and Robotics can actually be connected. Robotics is Physical AI, the skeleton and limbs of AI. Stablecoins are the infrastructure for value transfer. Prediction markets are information pricing mechanisms. AI trading is automated execution. Put these together, and you have a grand framework for the Agentic Economy. Add to that narratives like digital avatars, digital immortality, and AI proxy personalities, and this forms a comprehensive macro-level direction that can span both Web2 and Web3. I don't think the next cycle will revert to purely speculative logic, but I believe new and interesting things will emerge around the Agentic Economy. So, to summarize: the sentiment is indeed "finished," the old narrative is receding, but a new AI+Crypto macro-narrative may be forming. The key is whether we can find a new consensus after the collapse of the old one. Teddy: Reflections after the Consensus Conference — Lack of innovation is the root of the crisis; Agentic Economy may become the new consensus. Teddy: Let me start with a fundamental question. Before answering the question "Is the crypto world finished?", let's ask a question first—what is the core of the crypto world? I think the core is decentralization, including decentralized technology and the spirit of decentralization. These two points have always been valuable. So, essentially, decentralization cannot be finished; it is meaningful in the long run. But the reality is that the entire crypto world is facing a very serious problem—people feel it's useless. A large number of "useless assets" have been generated in the market, faith is waning, and funds and builders are leaving in large numbers. Of course, not everyone has left, but the rate of loss is very high. I've been thinking about the reasons. I think the core problem is the lack of genuine innovation in recent years. Looking back at history, Bitcoin was born in 2008, marking the first major innovation—a decentralized peer-to-peer electronic cash system. Over a decade of validation has proven its value. The second major innovation was Ethereum, which introduced the concepts of a "world computer" and smart contracts. This generated immense excitement at the time. The third was the DeFi Summer of 2020, which gave rise to a series of truly useful protocols such as Uniswap and Aave. To this day, these protocols remain the cornerstone of the entire on-chain ecosystem. It is precisely because of innovations like Bitcoin, Ethereum, and DeFi that we have today's RWA, the large-scale application of stablecoins, and the convergence of Web2 and Web3. However, in this cycle, we haven't seen a new product with the same impact. As a result, a large number of ineffective assets have appeared on the market, and many project teams no longer aim to create great products or build sustainable cash flow, but rather to "list on top exchanges." When listing becomes the sole objective, the industry will run into problems. This is because many projects stop building as soon as they are listed, lacking any real value. A typical alpha in this cycle has even become—shorting from the day the token is listed. Many tokens reach their highest point on the first day of listing, so who would be willing to hold them long-term? When an asset itself lacks long-term value, the market naturally loses confidence. Prices fall, and BTC, ETH, and DeFi tokens all come under pressure. Builders leave, VCs leave, incubators decrease, and the supply side decreases. The supply of truly high-quality assets is decreasing, which is a serious crisis. So where is the future? I've recently been focusing on prediction markets and AI. Prediction markets have value, but they haven't yet brought the same kind of shock as Bitcoin, Ethereum, and DeFi. What will truly make a difference is AI. For example, with current AI programming capabilities (Vibe Coding), you can generate a usable product from an idea in just over ten minutes. Previously, even a strong development team would need one to two weeks. This productivity improvement is a huge leap. Another example is the new generation of large language models, which not only "help you analyze problems," but can directly "help you solve problems." You say a sentence, and it executes it for you; this is already close to a true AI assistant or digital employee. Therefore, I strongly agree with the Agentic Economy direction. If Crypto is to achieve new consensus, it may have to combine with the Agentic Economy. Another direction is decentralized AI. Current AI is extremely centralized, with models controlled by a few companies and operating as a black box. In the future, there will likely be increasing emphasis on decentralized AI—a transparent, verifiable, and collectively owned model trained and inferred through decentralized networks. This direction is very early, but in the long run, it is highly consistent with the spirit of decentralization. If a democratic, open, transparent, and human-controlled AI system can be achieved, it will be a highly attractive long-term direction. In summary: The essence of the cryptocurrency market is not dead, but the lack of innovation has led to a crisis of faith. If we want to form a new "consensus" at the next Consensus Conference, then truly impactful new products must emerge. Currently, the most likely breakthrough lies in the Agentic Economy and decentralized AI. Leon: The Consensus Conference is clearly international, but the sectors are relatively quiet; AI trading and prediction markets may become the new direction for consensus. Leon: Because I'm based in Hong Kong, and we participated as a project team, I can share my observations and views on the industry from the perspective of an exhibitor. I think this Consensus Conference was quite international. CoinDesk's organizational capabilities are indeed excellent. While the overall foot traffic wasn't as large as at 2049, there were actually more overseas faces. Whether it was project teams, exhibitors, or attendees, Chinese and overseas people were roughly evenly split. We attended some Demo Days and clearly felt the international atmosphere. We also co-hosted a side event with Google, and most of the attendees were also from overseas. So, frankly, it's a bit of a shame; if this were a bull market, such a platform bridging Asia and the world would be incredibly powerful. We also met many overseas KOLs, channel partners, institutions, and even Wall Street investment bankers looking for projects. However, the problem is that this time it was indeed rather lukewarm. Not many project teams came; many people who came to our booth said there were very few interesting projects. Besides exchanges and service providers, there weren't many truly crypto-native projects seriously developing products; it was mostly service-oriented companies. Many people felt the ticket price wasn't worth it. Moreover, community KOLs rarely attended the event. Surprisingly, aside from us, there were almost no prediction market-related projects at the entire venue. Given how hot the prediction market sector is, there were virtually no related teams at this Consensus Conference. AI projects were also scarce, with only a few robotics-related projects. Another amusing point was that the hackathon's sponsor was a Base-based Meme Launchpad, but it was very quiet. Everyone got bored by the end of the hackathon, and even the first and second place winners left without staying to receive their prizes. This reflects that Meme is indeed no longer popular. Overall, the atmosphere of this bear market is quite strong, somewhat reminiscent of the wave in 2019. During the 2022 bear market, even with price drops, there were still many innovations, such as SocialFi and GameFi, and later Inscription and BRC-20, which still enjoyed popularity. However, in this current bull market, there hasn't been much real innovation, and the atmosphere has worsened since the bear market began. But I still think there are opportunities. I personally favor three areas. First, contracts, whether CEX or DEX perpetual contracts. The saying goes, "bull market spot trading, bear market contracts." Even in a bear market, there's still volatility, allowing for long and short positions and leverage. Trading contracts around major cryptocurrencies offers more opportunities than chasing short-lived coins like Meme or altcoins with 10-minute lifecycles. Moreover, a current trend is that stocks, commodities, and forex are all moving towards on-chain derivatives. Both exchanges and DEXs like Hyperliquid are starting to list these assets. Many traditional retail investors find it difficult to short gold or trade with leverage in the real world, but they can do so on-chain. This is the advantage of combining crypto with traditional assets. Secondly, there's AI. AI has been incredibly hot this year. Whether it's exploring protocol layers like ERC-8004 and EIP-402 within the industry, or deploying open-source LLMs and agents, everyone is involved. Outside the industry, AI is already disruptive in film, creation, and productivity. The key to the future is how AI can be combined with crypto. Thirdly, there's the prediction market. Prediction markets are still in their early stages, and people haven't yet found a way to participate on a large scale. But it's not just a game within the crypto world; institutions outside the crypto sphere are also watching. And since it's an on-chain application, those of us with on-chain experience have a natural advantage. Overall, I think that despite the strong bear market atmosphere, there's still a lot of interesting stuff that can be created around contracts, AI, and prediction markets. <p style="text-align:center"><strong mpa-from-tpl="t"><span leaf=""><img src="https://img.jinse.com.cn/133310_image3.png"/></span></strong></p><p style="text-align: left;"><strong><strong><span leaf="">To get Wu Shuo news and in-depth content, you can search for the Wu Shuo APP on the Apple/Wu Shuo official website; search for Wu Shuo audio and video on platforms such as Xiaoyuzhou; search for Wu Shuo communication on the Knowledge Planet platform; for media inquiries and business cooperation, please add WeChat customer service ipo19841984;</span></strong></strong><strong><strong><span leaf=""> How to set a starred account on the WuShuo WeChat Official Account; Experience the new WuShuo AI Assistant feature on the Official Account. data.title: Golden Morning Briefing | WLFI Becomes a Warning Signal for the Market; US Institutions Bullish on BTC
data.description:
Headline

▌Report: WLFI May Be an “Early Warning Sign” of a Crypto Market Collapse

According to a research report by data provider Amberdata, WLFI showed a significant early warning effect in the massive market liquidation on October 10, 2025. More than five hours before approximately $6.93 billion in leveraged positions were liquidated across the entire network, WLFI had already begun to fall sharply, while the price of Bitcoin remained around $121,000, showing no obvious pressure. The research suggests that because many trading platforms support WLFI as collateral, the token's sharp fluctuations led to a reduction in the value of the collateral, forcing traders to liquidate BTC and ETH to cover their positions, thus triggering a chain reaction across the entire market. Mike Marshall of Amberdata stated that this five-hour lead time is difficult to consider a coincidence, as WLFI's high leverage makes it more sensitive to market pressures.

Analysis: US Institutions Remain Bullish on BTC, While Overseas Investors Are Withdrawing. According to analysis by Greg Cipolaro, Head of Research at NYDIG, there is a divergence in sentiment between US institutional investors and offshore traders in the Bitcoin market. Currently, the annualized basis for CME Bitcoin futures is higher than that of the offshore exchange Deribit, indicating that US hedge funds and other institutions still prefer to pay a premium to maintain long positions, while interest in leveraged long-term exposure in the offshore market has clearly declined. Regarding previous market rumors that the "quantum computing threat" caused Bitcoin to fall to $60,000, NYDIG believes that the data does not support this logic. Recent Bitcoin price movements have shown a positive correlation with quantum computing-related stocks such as IONQ and D-Wave, rather than an inverse divergence. If quantum computing did indeed pose a targeted threat, related stocks should rise when Bitcoin falls. The current synchronized decline reflects a decrease in the market's overall risk appetite for long-term growth assets. Furthermore, Google Trends data shows that the increase in related searches usually coincides with rising rather than falling cryptocurrency prices, indicating that the topic is more driven by market enthusiasm than panic selling. data.title: How long does PayPal have left before AI takes over the "shopping path"?
data.description:
Author: LUKE SPILL, FintechBlueprint; Translation: Peggy, BlockBeats

As AI agents begin to replace humans in product discovery, decision-making, and order placement, the traditional e-commerce funnel is being rapidly compressed, and payment is no longer the end point of a transaction, but rather part of the embedded infrastructure. This article uses PayPal's acquisition of Cymbio as a starting point to analyze the new competitive landscape under the rise of Agentic Commerce: Google and Shopify are attempting to control the routing layer with UCP, OpenAI and Stripe are seizing the agent execution layer through ACP, and PayPal is striving to shift from a "payment button" to a key node in the "business workflow."

For fintech companies like PayPal and Stripe, the ability to embed the underlying protocols of AI commerce will determine whether they can continue to be at the table; and for the banking and crypto industries, the window of opportunity is equally short.

The following is the original text: Last week, PayPal acquired Cymbio, a platform that helps merchants complete sales across various AI interfaces, including Microsoft Copilot and Perplexity. Market sources estimate the deal is worth between $150 million and $200 million. This is widely seen as a key strategic move by PayPal to remain competitive in the Agentic Commerce sector. Therefore, as AI agents continue to compress and restructure the traditional e-commerce funnel, PayPal is shifting from a typical Web2 payment tool to more upstream and core business processes such as product discovery, catalog distribution, and order orchestration. This shift almost perfectly confirms our analysis from January of this year regarding exponential growth, the power law effect, and increasing returns to scale in Agentic Commerce. Meanwhile, the industry's infrastructure is rapidly taking shape: Google and Shopify are pushing the Universal Commerce Protocol (UCP); OpenAI and Stripe are collaborating on the Agentic Commerce Protocol (ACP); and Microsoft is embedding its checkout capabilities directly into Copilot. The shopping infrastructure centered around "machines," rather than "human users," is being rewritten at an unprecedented pace. Agentic Commerce is delivering on exponential growth expectations in a real-world way. The predictions from various parties are both astonishing and converging: McKinsey predicts that by the end of this decade, Agentic Commerce is expected to generate $1 trillion in revenue in the US retail market, accounting for about one-third of all online retail sales. Morgan Stanley predicts that by 2030, Agentic Commerce will drive U.S. e-commerce spending to $190 billion to $385 billion, representing a market penetration rate of 10%–20%. Bain predicts that by 2030, the Agentic Commerce market will reach $300 billion to $500 billion, accounting for approximately 15%–25% of total online retail sales. Existing adoption data indicates that we are at the inflection point of an exponential growth curve: by November 2025, 23% of US consumers had already used AI to complete a purchase. Cymbio may become PayPal's "middle layer" in AI commerce. For PayPal, Cymbio's potential role is as a middle infrastructure layer in its AI business system. Its core value proposition includes:

Synchronizing product catalogs across different markets and channels

Real-time inventory availability management

Roading orders to merchants' existing OMS (Order Management System) and fulfillment systems

Allowing merchants to continue acting as the legal entity (Merchant of Record) in transactions

Among them, the Store Sync product allows merchants' product catalogs to be directly discovered by AI agents such as Microsoft Copilot and Perplexity, and is expected to be integrated with ChatGPT and Google Gemini in the future.
The prerequisite for AI agents to complete transactions is that product data, prices, inventory, and fulfillment information must be machine-readable and highly reliable. From "Checkout" to "Agentic Commerce Workflow" PayPal processes over $1.7 trillion in payments annually, with over 142 million monthly active accounts. In the traditional model, PayPal's core leverage point is the moment the payment occurs. However, in the Agentic Commerce system, AI systems can handle product discovery, solution comparison, and even direct order placement for users, while PayPal handles identity verification and payment authorization. After integrating Cymbio, PayPal now covers the entire chain: Discovery: Products are recommended and presented by an AI agent. Decisioning: Options are continuously narrowed down through conversational interaction. Checkout: PayPal handles identity verification and payment. Fulfilment: Orders are directly injected into the merchant's system for execution. Protocol Battle: Service vs. Standards. While PayPal is advancing Agentic Commerce as a "product and service," Google and Shopify are building a cross-functional, standardized Agentic Commerce protocol system. The key point is: Google is embedding UCP (Universal Commerce Protocol) directly into Search and Gemini. Shopify, on the other hand, ensures that its millions of merchants only need to integrate once to reach multiple AI agents. This means that the underlying infrastructure of AI commerce is evolving from "single-point capabilities" to a "protocol-based network." The goal of UCP is to control the "routing layer" of AI commerce, rather than directly owning or operating the commerce itself. This is more of a defensive strategy: by making this layer a "free" public protocol and introducing strong network effects, it prevents any single competitor from monopolizing core control of the AI ​​business ecosystem. Therefore, PayPal is not directly competing with UCP, but rather actively embedding itself into this system. Google has explicitly stated that UCP-based checkout capabilities will support multiple payment service providers, including PayPal and Google Pay. In other words, UCP attempts to be a "neutral highway," while PayPal hopes to become an indispensable tollbooth and payment node on this highway. OpenAI and Stripe are the main competitors in this field. Back in September, Stripe and OpenAI announced the launch of Instant Checkout in ChatGPT, powered by the Agentic Commerce Protocol (ACP). ACP allows AI agents to proactively initiate purchase requests via a structured API, with Stripe issuing shared payment tokens to confirm payments under agent authorization. This enables AI, once authorized, to complete the entire transaction process on behalf of the user, from order placement to payment. Stripe subsequently launched the Agentic Commerce Suite in December 2025, enabling merchants to: Publish product catalogs for direct access by AI agents; and choose which agents to use. AI Agents Conduct Sales

Payments, Risk Control, and Dispute Resolution are Handled Through Stripe

Order Events are Reported Back to Existing Business Systems

Stripe processed over $1 trillion in payments in 2024, serving millions of businesses worldwide. Its competitive strategy is very clear: to become the "default wallet" and "action execution layer" for AI agents—a path highly similar to its early days as the default payment API for internet companies.

In this context, PayPal and Stripe are clearly clashing head-on:
The two are vying not only for payments themselves, but also for the key control points when AI agents actually "execute transactions."

Comparing the three systems together (This usually involves a horizontal comparison of UCP/ACP/PayPal + Cymbio: Who controls the routing layer, who controls the protocol, and who controls payment and fulfillment—and the sources of their respective network effects.) If you'd like, I can directly organize the next paragraph into a comparison table or a highly summarized "strategic assessment," clearly explaining the division of labor and game among the three parties.

Key Takeaways

Three impacts are particularly prominent:

Business transactions will become conversational and can be delegated.
Purchasing will no longer be a process where users click through steps, but rather AI will understand needs in conversation and complete the process on their behalf with authorization.

Merchants can "integrate once, distribute everywhere."
Merchants do not need to adapt to each platform individually; they only need to complete one integration, and their products can reach users through multiple AI agents and channels.

Payments will become embedded infrastructure, not just the endpoint of transactions. Payments will no longer be the "last step button," but a fundamental capability deeply embedded in the discovery, decision-making, and fulfillment processes. Payment networks' proactive approach: Incidentally, Mastercard announced in January 2026 that it was researching "AI business rules," essentially attempting to take the lead in defining the governance framework for this transformation. Payment networks clearly recognize that the power to set rules and standards will determine their future position before AI agents complete transactions on a large scale. As we pointed out in our analysis this January: banks, fintech companies, and the crypto industry must ensure they are "at the table," not incorporated afterward. If financial institutions fail to embed themselves in these platforms in advance, their financial functions may ultimately be internalized by Big Tech.

The Situation and Choices of Different Camps

For Banks

Traditional banks lack the technological infrastructure to directly compete with Google, OpenAI, or Microsoft at the Agentic Commerce level. However, they still possess three key resources: payment clearing channels, customer credit relationships, and compliance and regulatory experience.

These assets determine that banks will not disappear, but must reposition themselves.

For Fintech Companies

Companies such as PayPal, Stripe, and Adyen realized early on that simply providing payments is no longer sufficient to solidify their long-term position.

Therefore, they are actively moving upstream, entering: commerce orchestration, merchant services, and the infrastructure layer of the AI ​​era. For the crypto industry (For Crypto): The Agentic Commerce protocol system announced so far is almost entirely based on traditional financial pathways: credit cards, Google Pay, PayPal, Stripe, etc., occupy core positions. In UCP, ACP, and Store Sync, cryptocurrencies and stablecoins are largely absent, except for some sporadic experiments involving Stripe or Coinbase. Whether this is a significant strategic oversight or a deliberate exclusion remains to be seen. For crypto companies, the window of opportunity is very clear: if they can build a payment track natively adapted to AI agents (instant settlement, programmable money, global availability) and successfully embed it into an AI platform before the protocol is fully solidified, they have the potential to leapfrog traditional finance; otherwise, they may be permanently excluded from the system. In conclusion, PayPal is fundamentally trying to catch up with Stripe and adapt to rapidly changing consumer behavior. As people increasingly make daily decisions within AI platforms, these platforms will gradually evolve into "default virtual showcases" for brands. Whoever can embed themselves into the infrastructure behind these showcases will remain at the table. PayPal's stock price has been sluggish for some time, down about 37% from its 52-week high. Investors are increasingly questioning whether the company retains its structural relevance in the long term, and the rise of the Crypto + AI narrative has only exacerbated these concerns. Against this backdrop, the diversification around Agentic Commerce is not an aggressive move, but rather a "necessary cost" to maintain relevance. For PayPal, this is not icing on the cake, but an unavoidable entry fee: only by completing this shift can it remain at the core of the next generation of commerce infrastructure.

data.title: Polymarket vs. Kalshi: A Timeline of the Prediction Market Meme Battle
data.description:
Author: Hunter Ryerson, Pirate Wires; Translator: Peggy, BlockBeats

Editor's Note: From free grocery stores to a meme war of mutual mockery, the competition between Polymarket and Kalshi has long since transcended product and market share battles, becoming a meticulously crafted war for attention. This article traces the timeline of the rivalry between the two prediction market platforms over the years: regulatory battles, user bans and returns, "competitive meme-playing" on social media, and ultimately, a marketing spectacle that spills over into the real world. Behind the seemingly absurd and dramatic maneuvers lies a continuous rise in trading volume, valuation, and capital expectations.

The following is the original text:

February 12th, morning, Manhattan. You wake up in a cramped, shoebox-sized apartment that costs $2,000 a month, turn on the heat, and drag yourself to the pantry to see if you can scrape together some breakfast.

Then you remember: at 3 a.m. last night, you heated up and ate the last packet of instant ramen. Just as you're hesitating about whether to continue contributing 20% ​​of your income to DoorDash, a friend texts you, telling you about a new grocery store called "The Polymarket" that opened on Madison Street—and everything inside is free. So, naturally, you put on your pants, stroll over to Lower Manhattan, successfully squeeze into the store, and instantly enter "Black Friday shopping spree" mode, frantically buying everything in sight, grabbing everything your debt-ridden hands can reach. As you walk home with bulging shopping bags, stuffed full of Sour Patch Kids gummies and vegetables you haven't seen in weeks, tucked under your arm, you pass a billboard advertising a $50 free grocery credit at an East Village market sponsored by a company called "Kalshi." Am I dreaming? How could I be so lucky? Congratulations, you've been caught up in the latest wave of prediction market advertising. That's right. Just this week, the wildly popular prediction market platform Polymarket announced its latest marketing gimmick: a "completely free grocery store" in New York City, open from February 12th to February 16th. Meanwhile, its competitor, another prediction market, Kalshi, wasn't to be outdone, launching its own "grocery-themed" marketing campaign: giving away $50 in general grocery subsidies to everyone for one day at Westside Market on Third Avenue. This back-and-forth imitation prompted one user, X, to complain: "Kalshi and Polymarket can't even stand 24 hours a day; they copy everything." At first glance, it seems absurd that Polymarket and Kalshi would compete in marketing with "free bananas." But this is actually just the latest episode in the long-standing feud between the two platforms. It's important to understand that their weekly trading volume reaches billions of dollars. Their business model, simply put, is this: people can bet on the outcome of seemingly harmless events for extremely high returns—for example, whether the US military will arrest Venezuelan leader Nicolás Maduro while he's wearing casual pajamas. (That night, some unknown, but hopefully not Pete Hegseth, cyber spies made a fortune.) In short, in their short five-year history, these two prediction market platforms have always been competitors, but the real fierce competition has only fully erupted in the last two years. Polymarket, founded in 2020 by NYU dropout Shayne Coplan, is a cryptocurrency-based platform. Bettors need to deposit USDC, a stablecoin equivalent to the US dollar, on the Polygon blockchain to purchase "yes" or "no" prediction shares. In contrast, Kalshi initially operated almost entirely in US dollars, with transactions and fund deposits and withdrawals completed through traditional bank accounts. Since its launch in 2021, it has primarily focused on sports betting, which accounts for 90% of the platform's total trading volume. Polymarket, on the other hand, leans more towards geopolitical and cultural events, such as wars, conflicts, and elections, and even pays American influencers to promote its political content. From 2022 to 2025, due to strong regulatory pressure from the US Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) (and a $1.4 million fine), Polymarket temporarily banned US users from its platform, effectively handing over the US market to Kalshi for several years. However, just a few months ago, Polymarket returned to the US market, reigniting the fierce conflict between the two companies, with various frictions unfolding on the X platform and even in the wider cyberspace. For netizens who are "always online," the most entertaining part of this war is undoubtedly the so-called "competitive shitposting." In the sports world, this strategy manifests as parodies of official sports announcement cards—the kind you often see on ESPN or FOX Sports accounts used to announce trades, drafts, or injuries. Both platforms use attention-grabbing, joking headlines to "report" sports news. For example, Polymarket's "DICK IS GROWING" alludes to Toronto Raptors player Gradey Dick's weight gain; and Kalshi's "LOVES RECEIVING BALLS" refers to San Francisco 49ers running back Christian McCaffrey, saying he "likes to catch the ball"—you know, literally meaning "catching and passing the ball."

But as both sides fought for "meme-style attention," their tactics began to become more unscrupulous.

In November 2024, evidence emerged that Kalshi attempted to pay influencers—such as former NFL wide receiver and current X platform activist Antonio Brown—to publish and spread negative comments about Polymarket (see: "Kalshi Pays Influencers to Attack Polymarket CEO After FBI Raid"). In one case, a journalist was reportedly paid $3,500 to write a smear article against Polymarket.

(As an aside: If Solana were willing to pay me this much to write smear articles, I could impeach Jackie Fielder by Monday.) It's alleged that the influencers Kalshi "managed" have a combined following of millions. For the past few years, they've been trying to gradually erode Polymarket's credibility. Following this incident, the Trump administration relaxed regulations on prediction markets, allowing Polymarket to make a strong comeback in the US market. After months of preparation, the US user ban was finally lifted in December, and now Polymarket is trying to regain a majority share of the on-chain prediction market (currently, after integration with the Solana blockchain, Kalshi still holds a dominant position). One way they've found to "amplify their voice" is by publishing news on X. Over the past few months, Polymarket and Kalshi's brand accounts have been locked in a timeline battle, vying for the top spot with concise, powerful headlines and celebrity quotes—sometimes disregarding accuracy or any other principles. Recently, Polymarket mistakenly attributed a statement to Jeff Bezos and significantly exaggerated deportation data; while Kalshi spread false information about negotiations to acquire Greenland. Ultimately, this feud has spilled over from the online world into the real world, and hopefully, it will continue to have some positive "spillover effects" for ordinary Americans like you who are strapped for cash. But the real point is this: whether it's a marketing gimmick like "free groceries" or the mutual mockery and backstabbing on the X platform, both companies are extremely adept at generating buzz and keeping people talking about them. No matter how questionable their tactics to outdo each other—whether they seem shady or strangely generous—we're constantly discussing it. Perhaps this is exactly what they want. Kalshi and Polymarket are valued at $11 billion and $9 billion respectively, and they're still climbing at an astonishing rate. So, as long as the drama in this crazy war attracts a few hundred more bettors or a few more investors, it's all worth it. It's a win-win for these two "duelers," if you're willing to believe it.

Guest: Chase, former Binance listing manager; Podcast source: Class Representative at Attention; Compiled by: Deep Tide

Broadcast Date: February 10, 2026

Guest: Chase, former Listing Manager at Binance. During his tenure, he handled approximately 1000-2000 projects and personally oversaw the token launches of about 100 projects. The data he handled was of extremely high quality, representing a top-level perspective in the industry.

Core Insight: He believes the cryptocurrency world (especially the exchange environment) is a "non-dramatic" financial battlefield. Unlike the stock market, which is still cloaked in financial reports, it is rife with liquidity games and market maker manipulation. He observed that Bitcoin's candlestick charts can be manipulated by market makers to create perfect "artificial patterns" to declare their dominance.

Billion Dollar Chart (CFD) patterns can be drawn by market makers to claim perfect "artificial patterns."

Billion Dollar Chart (CFD) patterns can be manipulated to claim perfect dominance.

... In this market, information, traffic, attention, and even insider information are assets in themselves; as long as they have liquidity, they can be priced. * **Key Insights:** Macroeconomics and Crypto Valuation Logic: The Truth Behind the US Stock Market Bull Run: From the dot-com bubble to the present, the S&P 500 has been in a long-term bull market, but this may not be the case when compared to gold. The core reason for this phenomenon is the continuous expansion of the money supply. * **Asset Price Support:** Newly added money must find asset carriers. The growth of goods and services (GDP) cannot keep up with the rate of money issuance and credit growth. Excess money, besides forming bubbles, must flow into new asset classes. Cryptocurrencies, due to their low-cost issuance and lack of centralized verification, have become an excellent container for global overflow liquidity. • Cryptocurrency Valuation System: Unlike traditional finance which focuses on cash flow, the short-to-medium-term performance of cryptocurrency assets is determined by three factors: 1. Liquidity; 2. Attention/Traffic; 3. Tokenomics. Market Manipulation and "Market Maker" Mentality. • Can Bitcoin be manipulated? Yes. Chase observed Bitcoin rising for seven consecutive days with almost identical magnitude and trading volume in a certain week, followed by a sharp drop. This wasn't natural volatility, but rather a signal from large financial groups (market makers/market manipulators) to the market – "I control the rhythm; those who understand follow, those who don't get eaten up." • Everything can be priced: In the crypto world, information, traffic, and even insider information (such as Polymarket prediction markets) can be assetized. When most people reach a "consensus," it's often the moment for a reverse harvest. • A market without pretense: Exchanges like Binance vividly demonstrate what naked trading is. There are value investors here, but more often, there are market makers who see through liquidity and short-term profits; this is a financial battlefield where "the act is over."

  1. Frontier Trends: The Combination of AI Agents and Crypto

• The Measurement Challenge of AI: In the future, the economic scale generated by AI agents will exceed that of humans, but the existing GDP system cannot measure the micro-transactions of AI (API calls, data exchanges).

• Crypto as Infrastructure: Crypto is a financial infrastructure naturally suited for AI. It can provide measurement and settlement accurate to every tiny transaction, without the cumbersome trust processes of the banking system. Crypto may be for AI, not for humans.

  1. Industry Insights and Wealth Opportunities

• The Huge Profits of Stablecoins: Tron carries a large amount of USDT. Tether (the issuer of USDT) earns more in a year (approximately $15 billion) than the combined losses of OpenAI and Anthropic. This is a risk-free arbitrage model relying on the interest rate differential between US Treasury bonds, making it the industry's true "cash cow."

• Capital Equality: Crypto's biggest change in production relations lies in "capital equality." Regardless of who you are, as long as you meet the criteria, your funds can obtain equal returns (such as DeFi yields), breaking the traditional banks' discriminatory treatment of retail investors and high-net-worth clients.

• Capital Equality: Crypto's biggest change in production relations lies in "capital equality." Regardless of who you are, as long as you meet the criteria, your funds can obtain equal returns (such as DeFi yields), breaking the traditional banks' differential treatment of retail investors and high-net-worth clients.

• Career Advice:

  ◦ Regarding getting rich quick: This industry does indeed have a "spiritually rich" bonus; ordinary people who work hard (like taking advantage of airdrops) may earn more than in traditional manufacturing, but ultimately the big money flows to "Nascent Soul cultivators" (top players) or is redistributed.

  ◦ Advice for newcomers: First, understand two things—Bitcoin (as an asset allocation) and stablecoins (as a payment revolution). Don't just focus on speculation; see the opportunities brought about by the transformation of the payment system.

  ◦ 2026 Prediction: Bitcoin will rise, but possibly in ways you wouldn't expect (through pricing all information and liquidity games).
